actions.thought: I am holding 9s 2c (nine-deuce offsuit) on the Button/Small Blind. This is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although being on the Button gives me a positional advantage post-flop in heads-up play, the raw equity of this hand is too low to justify entering the pot, even for a limp. Since this is the first hand of the tournament, I have no reads on the opponent to justify an aggressive bluff. Saving chips is the prudent play here.

In heads-up play, the button (Small Blind) is the strongest position. 59s is a decent speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Raising is standard here to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. A 3x raise to 300 is a solid opening size to test the opponent's range while maintaining a favorable pot-to-stack ratio.

In a Heads-Up match, A9o is a strong hand, well ahead of the opponent's opening range on the button. Facing a 3x raise, I have good pot odds to call (need 33% equity). While a 3-bet is an option, calling is generally preferred to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid getting blown off the hand by a 4-bet. Being out of position suggests exercising pot control with a medium-strong hand like this.

I hold Top Pair with a 9 kicker on an A-Q-6 board. In a heads-up scenario, this is a strong hand. As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, the standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture (Ace-high, two broadways) hits the aggressor's perceived range well, so a continuation bet is likely. Checking allows me to call a bet to keep their bluffs in and avoid bloating the pot unnecessarily against better Aces. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or strong draws while folding out the bluffs I want to catch.

The flop Qd-6s-As favors my range as the pre-flop raiser significantly. While I have completely missed with 9c-5c, a continuation bet is standard here to represent an Ace or a Queen and force the opponent to fold their air or weak middle pairs. A small sizing (1/3 pot) is efficient on such a dry/heavy board to exert pressure without overcommitting.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a 9 kicker on an As-Qd-6s board. The opponent, who raised pre-flop on the button, has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot). This board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor, so a c-bet is expected with a wide range including value hands, draws, and bluffs. My hand is too strong to fold. Raising would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot and isolate me against better Aces or two-pair hands. Calling is the most prudent play to control the pot size out of position, keep the opponent's range wide, and allow them to continue bluffing on later streets.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a 9 kicker. The turn brings the King of Clubs, making the board As-Qd-6s-Kc. This card significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, connecting with hands like AK, KQ, KJ, and completing a straight for J10. As the Big Blind playing out of position, I should check. Leading out would be ill-advised as better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will fold. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff, so checking to see if the opponent continues their aggression is the standard line.

The King of Clubs is a significant scare card that strengthens my perceived range as the pre-flop and flop aggressor. Having already bet the flop, continuing on the turn allows me to represent high-equity hands such as Ace-King, King-Queen, or even the nut straight (Jack-Ten). Since Pro has only check-called thus far, their range likely consists of marginal Queens or weak Aces that will struggle to call a second, larger barrel on such a coordinated and heavy board. A bet of 600 (60% of the pot) exerts maximum pressure to force a fold from these medium-strength hands.

The turn brings the King of Clubs, making the board [Qd 6s As Kc]. This is a difficult card as it interacts heavily with the pre-flop aggressor's range, completing straights (J10) and making two pairs (AK, KQ). The opponent fires a second barrel of 600 into 1000 (60% pot). While my hand (Top Pair, 9 kicker) has shrunk in relative strength, folding top pair in a heads-up match to a turn bet is generally too exploitable, given the opponent can be barreling with flush draws (spades) or semi-bluffs. Raising achieves nothing as it isolates me against better hands. The correct play is to call (bluff-catch) and re-evaluate on the river, aiming to get to showdown if the river bricks.

The river brings the 9d, improving my hand to Two Pair (Aces and Nines). However, the board [Qd 6s As Kc 9d] is very coordinated; J10 makes a straight, and hands like AK, AQ, and sets still beat me. Leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to get value from worse hands (which will fold) and opens me up to raises from better hands. Checking allows me to induce bluffs from missed spade draws or see a showdown against one-pair hands like AJ or AT that might check back. Given the strength of the 3-barreling range, checking and evaluating is the optimal line.

On the river, I have improved to a pair of nines. While this is a weak hand on a board containing an Ace, King, and Queen, it does possess some marginal showdown value against missed draws (like a spade flush draw) or lower pairs (like a six). Pro has check-called three streets, which suggests they have a piece of the board but are playing it cautiously. Since it is unlikely a bet would fold out an Ace, King, or even a Queen, and my pair of nines is now strong enough to beat a total air bluff, checking back is the most prudent play to minimize loss and see the opponent's hand early in the tournament.

In Heads-Up play, Q7s is a strong candidate for an open raise from the Button (Small Blind). It has good playability post-flop due to flush and straight potential. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately if the opponent folds weak holdings, or play a pot in position with a playable hand. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size to balance my range and offer good pot odds while still charging the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind holding 63o. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, meaning it is 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300. While the pot odds are 3:1, 63o is one of the weakest starting hands and realizes equity very poorly out of position. Given that we are early in a 150-hand match, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for better spots rather than bloating a pot with a bottom-tier hand while out of position.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy is to open-raise to around 3x the big blind to take control of the betting lead and build a pot with a hand that likely has significant equity over the opponent's range.

I hold AQs, a premium hand in heads-up play. The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb). Being out of position, I have the option to 3-bet for value or flat call to keep the opponent's range wide and protect my calling range. Given the larger 3x open size and deep stacks (85bb effective), flat calling is a prudent strategy to control pot size while out of position and avoid facing a 4-bet. I will call to see a flop.

I hold Top Two Pair (Aces and Queens) on a wet board (8c Ah Qc). As the pre-flop caller out of position, it is standard to check to the pre-flop raiser. This board texture hits the opponent's range well (containing Ax, broadways, and club draws), increasing the likelihood they will c-bet. Checking allows me to induce bluffs or get value from their betting range (top pair, draws). If I lead out, I risk folding out their bluffs. My hand is strong enough to check-raise or check-call depending on the bet size, but the first step is to check.

I have top pair with an Ace and a nine kicker on an Ace-high board. This is a strong position in a heads-up pot. After the opponent checked, I should make a continuation bet to gain value from weaker Aces, Queens, or various draws like clubs or straight draws. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard size that balances building the pot and managing risk.

I hold Top Two Pair (AsQs) on a wet board of 8c Ah Qc. The opponent c-bet 300 (half-pot). While I block some of the opponent's value range (Ax, Qx), there are still many hands they can have that connect with this board, such as flush draws (clubs), straight draws (JT, KJ), and weaker Ax/Qx hands. In Heads-Up play, it is crucial to extract value with strong hands and charge draws while the board is dynamic. A check-raise here builds the pot for value and denies equity to floats or cheap draws. Slow-playing is risky due to the flush draw and potential straight runouts. I will raise to 1100, which is roughly 3.6x the opponent's bet, to maximize value and charge draws effectively.

I have top pair with a mediocre kicker (9) on a coordinated board (Ace-high with two clubs and straight possibilities). The opponent's check-raise is a strong signal, representing potentially a better Ace (AJ, AT), two pair (A8, Q8), or a set (88). However, it could also be a semi-bluff with a flush draw or a straight draw. Folding top pair heads-up at this stage is too conservative, but raising would likely only be called by hands that beat me. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, see how the turn develops, and catch potential bluffs while minimizing losses against stronger made hands.

The turn card is the 2c, which completes the flush draw present on the flop. Since the opponent called my check-raise on the flop, their range includes a significant number of flush draws, as well as strong top pairs and straight draws. While I still hold Top Two Pair, the board texture has shifted to favor the opponent's draw-heavy range. Leading out here is risky; if I am raised, I am in a difficult spot against a likely flush. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot, avoid being blown off my hand, and potentially induce bluffs or get to a cheaper showdown.

I have top pair with a 9 kicker on a board where the flush draw just completed with the 2 of clubs. Pro check-raised the flop, indicating strength, but has now checked the turn. This check could be a trap with a completed flush or a set, or it could be a sign of caution given the flush possibility. Checking behind is the most prudent play to control the pot size, realize my equity, and see the river card for free without risking a check-raise that would force me to fold top pair.

The river card is the 9c, putting four clubs on the board (8c, Qc, 2c, 9c). While I hold Top Two Pair (AsQs), I do not hold a club. Any club in the opponent's hand gives them a flush. The opponent called my check-raise on the flop (which had two clubs), meaning their range is heavy on flush draws and strong value hands. Although they checked back the turn, the arrival of the fourth club significantly devalues my hand relative to the potential nuts. Betting here has no value, as worse hands (non-club Ax) will fold, and better hands (any flush) will call or raise. My best strategy is to check and hope to reach a cheap showdown against a hand like AK or AJ without a club, or evaluate if the opponent bets.

The board has four clubs on it, meaning any single club in my opponent's hand makes a flush. I have Two Pair (Aces and Nines), but I do not hold a club. While I improved to Two Pair on the river, its value is significantly diminished by the four-flush texture. Pro check-raised the flop, showing strength, but then checked the turn and river as the flush possibilities increased. It is highly likely that Pro either has a marginal hand without a club or is potentially trapping. There is no value in betting here because a worse hand is unlikely to call, and I would be forced to fold to a check-raise. Checking back allows me to get to showdown and potentially win against a missed draw or a weaker Ace without further risk.

In a Heads-Up match, while ranges are wide, 54 offsuit is still near the bottom of the starting hand distribution. Despite having the Button and a stack lead, the hand lacks high card strength or suited potential to justify a raise or a limp against a competent opponent. Folding is the standard disciplined play here to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.
KTo is a strong hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and exert pressure on Pro.

I hold 4c 3c in the Big Blind facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button. While suited connectors have playability, 43s is at the bottom of the range. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), requiring 33% equity. However, being out of position makes it difficult to realize full equity, and 43s suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated flushes/straights). Against a 3x open, this is a standard fold.

In Heads-Up play, the Small Blind acts as the Button. Holding a pocket pair of Sixes (66) is a strong hand, well above the median winning hand. I want to build the pot and seize the initiative. Limping allows the Big Blind to see a free flop with random holdings that could out-flop me. A min-raise to 200 is a standard, balanced opening size that puts pressure on the opponent while keeping the pot manageable if 3-bet. Given the effective stack depth of 75 big blinds, I am raising for value and to deny equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 2s, which is a very weak hand. The opponent has raised to 200 from the Button. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, 92o has extremely poor playability post-flop and often results in losing more chips when hitting a marginal piece of the board. Given the stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

7s 4d is a very weak starting hand, even in a heads-up situation. Since I am at a stack disadvantage against a 'Pro' opponent, there is no need to play such low-equity cards. Folding here is the standard professional play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button (SB) with Q6o. In heads-up play, this hand has decent equity (~52%) against a random hand. Looking at history, the opponent (Flash) has folded to my min-raises from the Button in Hand #3 and Hand #7. Since Q6o is a marginal hand that benefits from fold equity but plays reasonably well in position if called, a min-raise to 200 is the best play to attack the opponent's blinds and capitalize on their tendency to fold to small raises.

I am in the Big Blind with J4o. Pro (the button) has raised to 200. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, J4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. I have already lost some significant chips in earlier hands (Hand #2 and Hand #4) and prefer to wait for better spots to challenge Pro's wide opening range rather than defending with the bottom of my range.

In heads-up play, T7o is a standard opening hand from the button. I want to maintain an aggressive posture and take advantage of my positional edge. Opening for 3x the big blind (300) has been my standard sizing in previous hands and exerts pressure on the Big Blind while allowing me to control the pot size.

The opponent opens to 3x (300 chips) from the Button/SB. While T5s has decent raw equity against a wide heads-up range, the larger opening size reduces my pot odds to 33% and lowers the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR), making it harder to realize equity out of position. T5s is near the bottom of the defending range against a 3x open. Without a read that the opponent is opening too wide or playing weak post-flop, folding is the disciplined play to avoid bleeding chips in a bloated pot with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. In heads-up play, a King is a reasonable hand to play. Looking at the history (Hands 3, 7, and 9), the opponent 'Flash' has folded every time I have min-raised from the Small Blind. This suggests they are playing tight out of the Big Blind or respect the raise. Since K7o has decent equity and the opponent is over-folding to aggression, raising is the most profitable play to attempt to steal the pot immediately or play the hand in position with the initiative. I will continue with the standard min-raise size of 200.

Pocket sixes are a strong hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of the Button's wide opening range. I will 3-bet to 600 to take the initiative and extract value. This also helps define the opponent's range and potentially ends the hand pre-flop.

I opened with K7o, a standard button raise in heads-up. The opponent (Flash) has 3-bet to 600. In previous hands (Hands 3, 7, 9), Flash folded to every single one of my opens. This sudden aggression after a string of folds strongly indicates a narrow, value-heavy range (likely strong Aces, good Kings, or pairs). K7o performs poorly against such a range, often being dominated. Although I have position and the price isn't terrible (need 33% equity), the likelihood that I am well behind makes calling -EV. Without a read that they are 3-betting light, the best play is to respect the strength and fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Qs7s, a suited gapper that plays reasonably well in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, has a larger stack. Given the blinds of 50/100 and the current pot of 150, I want to apply pressure and take control of the hand. A standard 3x open (300 total) is appropriate here to either win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop scenario with initiative and a hand that has decent equity and playability.

I am in the Big Blind with T8o. The opponent on the Button raises to 300 (3bb). This 3x sizing is larger than the standard min-raise, offering me worse pot odds (need 33% equity realization). While T8o has raw equity of about 45-47% against a wide opening range, playing this hand out of position without the initiative against a larger opening size is difficult and often -EV due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. I have folded similar or slightly weaker hands (T5s) to this sizing previously. Maintaining my chip lead by avoiding marginal spots out of position against 3x opens is a prudent strategy.

In Heads-Up play, Q2s is within the standard opening range (top ~40-45% of hands), as it has flush potential and a high card. While the kicker is weak, position (Button) allows for easier post-flop navigation. Although the opponent 3-bet my last button raise (Hand 11), my raises have taken down the pot pre-flop 3 out of 4 times (75% fold equity). Given this high success rate, raising remains a profitable play to steal the blinds. I will open with a standard min-raise. If faced with significant aggression (3-bet), I can easily fold this marginal hand.

Ten-Deuce offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call the raise, the hand has very poor playability post-flop, especially when out of position against a competent opponent. I prefer to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Holding 3c 4d on the button, this is one of the weakest hands in the range. While I have had success stealing blinds previously, Pro has shown a willingness to play back or fold selectively. Given my stack size relative to the blinds and the poor equity of 34o, I will fold and wait for a more advantageous hand to maintain stack stability.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-2 offsuit, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having a significant chip lead and heads-up ranges being wider, this hand has very poor playability and equity. Attempting to steal the blind here is risky if the opponent defends or 3-bets, and playing a bloated pot with 9-high is rarely profitable. The standard and most disciplined play is to fold and forfeit the small blind.

I am on the button with Qd2d. This is a suited hand with some playability, although the kicker is weak. In a heads-up situation, suited hands are worth opening or completing the small blind to see a flop. I have 7,600 chips (76 BBs), and the opponent has 12,250 chips. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and maintain an aggressive image, as several previous hands have shown the opponent folding to pre-flop raises.

I hold KJo in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. In heads-up play, KJo is a very strong hand, well ahead of the opponent's wide opening range. I am getting 3:1 pot odds on a call (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600). While I will be out of position post-flop, the hand is too strong to fold and strong enough to defend. A 3-bet is possible, but calling keeps the opponent's range wide and manages the pot size.

I hold King-Jack offsuit on a paired flop of 5c-5h-3c. I am out of position as the pre-flop caller. The board texture is relatively dry except for the flush draw, but generally favorable for the pre-flop aggressor to c-bet. My King-high has showdown value against a wide button opening range, but leading out (donk betting) is generally not optimal here as it allows the opponent to play perfectly against me. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking creates no liability and keeps the pot controlled.

On this 5-5-3 flop, the board is relatively static. I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw with my Qd2d. The 'Pro' has checked to me. Given that I was the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a bet to represent a pair, a five, or simply to force a fold from many hands in the opponent's range that missed this flop entirely. A bet of around 1/2 to 2/3 pot is standard here. I will bet 300 to maintain pressure.

I hold King-Jack offsuit on a paired board of 5c-5h-3c. The opponent has c-bet 300 into a pot of 600. In a heads-up match, ranges are wide, and King-high frequently has showdown value against a button opening range that includes many flush draws, straight draws, and pure air. The half-pot bet size offers me 3:1 odds, requiring roughly 25% equity to continue. With two clean overcards (6 outs to top pair) and the likelihood that King-high is currently the best hand against his bluffs, folding is too weak. Raising would isolate me against better hands, so calling is the most prudent line to keep his bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold King-Jack offsuit on a board of 5c-5h-3c-7h. I called the flop c-bet with King-high, aiming to catch bluffs or hit an overcard. The turn 7h brings a second flush draw (hearts) and connects with some straight draws (4-6), but does not drastically change the board texture for most of the opponent's range. Donk betting here would serve no purpose as it folds out bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate if the opponent fires a second barrel. Checking is the standard line to keep the opponent's range wide.

On the turn 7h, the board remains relatively low and double-paired. While I was the aggressor on the flop, Pro's call suggests they have some connection to the board, a pocket pair, or a draw. The 7h is a card that doesn't significantly change the board texture or improve my Qd2d. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and see a free river card. I still have some showdown value against complete air and can evaluate the river based on the final card and Pro's action.

I hold King-high (Kh Js) on a board of 5c-5h-3c-7h-8s. After calling the flop c-bet, the turn went check-check, suggesting the opponent has a capped range, likely containing Ace-high, weak pairs, or missed flush/straight draws. My hand has showdown value against the missed draws but loses to Ace-high and any pair. Turning this hand into a bluff by betting is risky as the opponent is likely to call with Ace-high or better in a heads-up scenario. Checking allows me to realize my equity against his absolute air (busted draws) for free or evaluate if he decides to bet the river. Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size.

On the river 8s, the board is 5c-5h-3c-7h-8s. I have Queen-high (Qd2d). Pro has checked to me after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. While my Queen-high has very little showdown value, it could potentially beat missed draws like 6-4 or various club draws. However, Pro's check-call on the flop suggests they likely have a piece of the board or at least Ace-high. Betting now to bluff them off a pair of 3s, 7s, 8s, or even Ace-high is risky given the tournament's long-term nature. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free and conserve my stack, as Queen-high is unlikely to fold out better hands that have checked twice.

In heads-up play, Queen-Jack offsuit is a strong hand well within the top range of starting hands. Position on the button allows me to dictate the action. Looking at the history, I have been consistently opening to 200 (min-raise), and the opponent 'Flash' has folded frequently to these raises (Hands 3, 7, 9, 13). Raising to 200 applies pressure, exploits the opponent's fold frequency, and builds a pot with a strong hand in position.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o. Pro has raised to 200 (a 2x open). While the price to call is only 100 more, T4o is a very weak hand to play out of position. Given the current stack dynamics and my previous tendency to fold weak hands to these opens, I will continue to play tight and fold this hand to wait for a more favorable spot.

Holding KTo on the button in a heads-up situation is a strong hand that should be raised for value. The opponent has folded to several of my 3x raises recently (Hands 6, 10, 12), and raising allows me to take initiative or win the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with T9o facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). Flash has been opening frequently to this size. I need to call 200 chips to win a pot of 400, offering me 2:1 pot odds. T9o has sufficient equity (approx 47% against a wide opening range) and playability to defend. Folding would be too tight given the wide range, and 3-betting is not necessary with this specific holding. I will call and play post-flop.

On a dry Q-4-3 rainbow board, T9o has missed completely, holding only two overcards to the low cards and no draws. The board texture favors the pre-flop raiser (Flash), who has shown a tendency to c-bet frequently. As the Big Blind out of position with a weak hand, the standard play is to check. There is no value in leading out (donk betting) as it unlikely folds out better hands and builds a pot with poor equity. I will check and likely fold to a continuation bet.

As the pre-flop aggressor on a dry Queen-high board, I have a significant range advantage. King-Ten has decent equity and overcards if called, but a continuation bet is the standard play here to capitalize on the board texture which likely missed the opponent's defending range. A half-pot bet should be sufficient to fold out most non-pairing hands.

On a dry Q-4-3 rainbow flop, my hand (T9o) has missed completely, leaving me with only Ten-high and no draws. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has fired a continuation bet of half the pot (300 into 600). This board texture favors their range, and they have shown a tendency to c-bet frequently. I am out of position with very low equity (requiring ~25% pot odds but likely having less against a continuing range) and no clear path to improve. Floating or bluff-raising would be specious without at least some backdoor equity or a draw. The most profitable play is to fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I am on the Button with T3o, which is in the bottom 10-15% of starting hands. While the opponent has been folding frequently to my button raises (allowing for a wide opening range), T3o is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop if called. I have previously folded hands like 4h5d and 2c9s, and T3o is weaker or comparable to those. To maintain a balanced frequency and not over-extend with the absolute bottom of my range, I will fold and concede the small blind.

I am holding AsJs, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I have the button and want to put pressure on the opponent while building a pot for value. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size in this tournament, and it keeps my range balanced.

I hold 7d 2h, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The opponent, acting as the Small Blind/Button, has opened with a 3x raise to 300. Although the opponent is aggressive and opens frequently, calling 200 more chips into a pot of 600 requires roughly 33% equity, and 72o barely meets this threshold even against a random hand, while suffering from terrible playability and being out of position. Defending here is negative EV. The prudent play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I hold A3o on the Button (SB). In heads-up play, any Ace is a strong candidate to open-raise due to card removal and high-card value. Looking at the hand history, the opponent 'Flash' has folded to my min-raises 5 out of 6 times (Hands 3, 7, 9, 13, 17). This indicates they are over-folding their Big Blind to aggression. Therefore, a min-raise is highly profitable both as a value play with an Ace and as a steal attempt. I will raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four of hearts. Pro has raised to 2x (200) from the button. I have already posted 100, so it is only 100 more to call into a pot of 300. I need approximately 20% equity to justify a call, and T4s performs well enough against a wide button opening range to defend. I will call to see a flop.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a weak kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw on a King-high board. Since I defended from the Big Blind pre-flop, checking to the initial raiser is the standard play here. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Pro reacts to this board. My hand is strong enough to call a standard continuation bet, but not strong enough to lead out into the aggressor.

I hold A3o on a K-T-2 rainbow flop. This is a dry board that hits my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor (possessing AK, KQ, AA, KK, TT), but my specific hand is effectively Ace-high showdown value. Betting here has little utility: it folds out hands I currently beat (like 87s, 54s) and gets called mostly by hands that beat me (Kx, Tx, pocket pairs). By checking, I pot-control, realize my equity for free, and keep the opponent's range wide, potentially inducing bluffs on later streets or allowing me to get to showdown cheaply. I plan to check back and re-evaluate on the turn.

The Ace of Clubs on the turn is a significant card that likely favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, even though they checked back on the flop. My pair of Tens is now third pair. Since the flop went check-check, it's possible Pro was pot-controlling a marginal hand or missed entirely. However, leading out here doesn't achieve much; better hands won't fold, and worse hands will rarely call. I will check again to see Pro's reaction. If Pro bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent way to manage the pot with a marginal showdown value hand.

I have improved to top pair on the turn with the Ace of Diamonds, but my kicker (3) is the lowest possible. The board [Kh 2d Ts Ac] is somewhat coordinated, with QJ completing a straight. Betting here has mixed utility: I might extract value from a King (Kx), but better Aces and straights effectively have me crushed, and worse hands I beat (like underpairs or Tens) may simply fold to the Ace scare card. Checking back allows me to pot-control, avoid a check-raise, and keep the opponent's range wide to potentially induce a bluff on the river or extract value on a safe river card.

The board has checked through on both the flop and the turn. I have a pair of Tens, which is currently third pair on an Ace-King high board. Given the passive action so far, my hand has significant showdown value. On the turn, the board pairs with the Deuce of Diamonds (6s Kd 2s 2d). My hand (Ah 3c) still holds showdown value as Ace-high. My opponent has checked twice, indicating likely weakness or a draw (spades or now diamonds). Betting here has little utility: it folds out worse hands (like Q-high) that have very little equity against me, and isolates me against better hands (pairs) or strong draws that might check-raise. Given my short stack (approx 19.5 BBs), pot control is paramount. I want to reach showdown cheaply. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and potentially induce a bluff on the river or win at showdown against missed draws.

On the river, the board is 6s-Kd-2s-2d-8d. Both players checked the flop and turn after Pro raised pre-flop. This passive line from the pre-flop aggressor often suggests they have a hand with some showdown value but not enough to bet for value (like a small pair or a 6), or they have completely given up with air. My Ace-high has significant showdown value in heads-up play, but it's unlikely to get called by worse if I bet. By checking, I can potentially win the pot at showdown for free or see if Pro attempts a late bluff with a weaker hand. Given Pro's short stack, I will check and likely call a small bet or simply take it to showdown.

The board is Kd 6s 2s 2d 8d. I hold Ah 3c (Ace-high). The opponent (Flash) has checked on the Flop, Turn, and River. This triple-check line indicates weakness, likely a missed spade draw, a weak pair (6x or 8x), or a lower high card. My Ace-high has decent showdown value against missed draws and random air. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (which will fold) and risks being called by weak pairs that I am currently losing to but can't force out. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity in this small pot and preserves my stack (19.5 BB) in a tournament situation where chip preservation is key.

I am on the button with 2s3c. This is a very weak hand, but I have a massive chip lead over 'Pro' (17800 vs 2050). In heads-up play, maintaining pressure is important, but 23o is generally a fold or a limp-fold candidate even with a stack advantage. However, given the blinds are 50/100 and I only need to put in 50 more to see a flop, and considering I want to keep the pressure on the short stack, I could call to see a cheap flop. But realistically, with such a poor hand, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid giving the short stack any unnecessary double-up opportunities or momentum, even if the cost is small. Checking the history, 'Pro' has been folding to raises and playing cautiously. I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot to exploit my stack lead.

I hold Ace-Ten suited (Ah Th) on the Button (SB), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My stack is approximately 21.5 big blinds (2150 chips). I have position post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is the standard aggressive play to build the pot for value while maintaining a balanced range, as I have used this sizing with various hand strengths previously. If the opponent 3-bets, I have a strong enough hand to defend or shove.

I hold 8s 9s in the Big Blind, a strong suited connector for heads-up play. Pro is short-stacked with only 2,000 chips total. Pro opened for a minimum raise to 200. I only need to call 100 more to see the flop in a 400-chip pot. Given my massive chip lead and the equity of my hand, calling is the most standard play to see a flop and potentially outplay or stack the opponent post-flop without the variance of a pre-flop 3-bet jam.

I have bottom pair on a very coordinated board containing an Ace and a Queen, plus a flush draw. This board likely connects well with my opponent's opening range. With a marginal hand like a pair of nines, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot and see how the opponent chooses to proceed. Given the opponent's short stack of 1,800 remaining chips, I want to avoid committing more chips unnecessarily on such a high-card heavy board.

I hold top pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker and the nut flush draw on a wet board of Qh-Ac-9h. While this is a very strong hand, checking back is a strategic play I have employed frequently in this match with top pair (e.g., Hands 70, 74). By checking, I protect my checking range, control the pot size at a 20BB stack depth, and induce the opponent to bluff or value bet worse hands on later streets. I also hold the Ah, which blocks the nut flush draw, making it safer to give a free card. I plan to call down or value bet on future streets depending on the runout.

I have third pair (nines) on a board with an Ace and a Queen. Pro checked back the flop, which could indicate a weak range or a draw. Since my hand has showdown value but is vulnerable, I will check again to control the pot size and see if the opponent bets or checks behind. With the opponent's short stack, I want to avoid unnecessary risk unless I improve.

I hold top pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker and the nut flush draw. Checking back the flop was a pot control play to disguise my hand strength. On the turn, I have an opportunity to bet for value, but given my stack depth (20BB) and the previous history of passive lines with top pair (e.g., Hand #74), checking back again is a strong option. It further under-represents my hand, potentially inducing the opponent to bluff the river or call a value bet on the river with a wider range. It also eliminates the risk of facing a check-raise on the turn. I plan to call any reasonable bet on the river or value bet if checked to.

I have third pair (nines). The action has been checked through on every street since the flop. The board contains an Ace and a Queen, but the lack of betting suggests neither player has a strong piece of it. My nines are likely the best hand, but betting doesn't accomplish much against a short-stacked opponent who might fold everything I beat and only call with better 9s or better. Checking allows me to reach showdown safely and win the pot.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker on a board of Qh-Ac-9h-5d-2c. After raising pre-flop, I checked back the flop and turn for pot control and to disguise my hand strength, consistent with my previous plan. The opponent has checked all three streets, strongly suggesting a capped range consisting of weak pairs (like Qx, 9x) or missed draws. The river 2c is a brick that changes nothing. Because I have under-represented my hand, the opponent may perceive my range as weak (missed draws or low pairs) and call a bet with a worse hand. Therefore, I will make a thin value bet of half the pot (200) to extract value from Queens or Nines, as planned in my previous thoughts.

On a board of Qh-Ac-9h-5d-2c, I hold third pair with 9s8s. The action was checked through on the flop and turn, which typically indicates that neither player has a strong hand like a high Ace or a Queen. Pro's river bet of 200 into a 400-chip pot (1/2 pot) looks like either a thin value bet with a weak pair or a bluff with a missed draw (like hearts). Since I only need to be right 25% of the time to justify the call (getting 3:1 pot odds) and my hand is a very credible bluff-catcher in a pot that was checked twice, calling is the most mathematically and strategically sound decision.

I have a weak hand (Q3o) on the button, but I am in a heads-up situation with a massive chip lead. My opponent, Pro, is down to 2500 chips, while I have 17350. By raising to 300 regularly, I put a lot of pressure on him and force him to play for his stack or fold. Since the blinds are 50/100, a raise to 300 is consistent with my previous aggressive play which has been effective at chipping him down.

I hold 7s 2h, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em (7-2 offsuit). My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 from the button. I am in the Big Blind with a stack of 2500 (25 BBs). While Flash is raising frequently, calling 200 chips (8% of my stack) with 7-2 offsuit out of position is a losing play. The equity of this hand is too low to justify a call, even with pot odds, and playing it post-flop is difficult. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Current stack is 24.5 BBs. Q8o is a reasonable hand to open from the button in heads-up play (top ~42% of hands). Given the stack depth, a min-raise is appropriate to apply pressure and steal the blinds while leaving room to fold to a 3-bet if necessary. I have successfully raised similar or worse hands (like Qh4h in Hand 107) recently. Folding here would be too passive given the need to maintain stack size against a large chip leader.

I have been dealt a premium hand, Pocket Queens, and I am facing a raise from a very short-stacked opponent. With the opponent's stack at only 23 big blinds, my goal is to maximize value and ideally get all the chips in. A 3-bet to 600 is a standard sizing that puts significant pressure on the opponent while allowing for a shove or a call to a variety of hands in their opening range. I am never folding this hand given the current stack depths.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after opening on the Button with Q8o. My stack is effectively 23 BBs. The opponent's raise size is to 600, requiring 400 more to call into a pot of 800 (getting 3:1 odds). While I have position post-flop, Q8o performs poorly against a standard 3-betting range (often dominated by AQ, KQ, QJ, 88+) and lacks the playability of suited connectors or pairs. Calling would commit nearly 20% of my remaining stack, creating a low SPR situation with a marginal hand. Preserving my 21 BB stack for better opportunities is the prudent play here.

I am on the button with a decent connectors hand (9h, 8s). Pro is currently sitting on a short stack of 2,200 chips (22 BB). Historically, Pro has been folding to my 3x opens from the button quite frequently. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on his stack and pick up the blinds, or see a flop in position if he calls. If he decides to shove, I will have to evaluate the math, but for now, the aggressive play is the most profitable strategy against a short stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 22bb effective. The opponent on the Button (huge chip leader) raises to 3bb (300). While I get 3:1 pot odds, T7o is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a larger-than-minimum raise size. With a stack of 22bb, I need to be mindful of preserving chips for better spots to reshove or defend with higher equity realization. Although I defended T7o once before (Hand 82), it resulted in a check-fold on the flop. The marginal nature of the hand combined with the positional disadvantage and the 3x raise size makes folding the most +EV decision here.

I hold 2h 6s in the Small Blind (Button) with a stack of 21.5 BB. This is an extremely weak hand (bottom 10% of hands). Although I have position post-flop, my opponent has a massive chip lead and has shown a willingness to 3-bet or defend. With 21.5 BB, I need to preserve chips and pick better spots to enter the pot or steal. Playing 62o is essentially burning chips in the long run. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I hold a massive chip lead over my opponent, who is currently playing a short stack of about 20 big blinds. My hand, 6s 2d, is very weak, but my opponent has shown a high frequency of folding to pre-flop raises in recent hands. I will maintain the pressure by raising to 300, aiming to pick up the blinds without seeing a flop. If they shove, I can easily fold given the small percentage of my stack at risk.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 3s, a very weak hand (6-high offsuit). The opponent on the Button has raised to 300 (3bb). I have 20.5 big blinds remaining. The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 400, requiring 33% equity to break even, but realizing that equity out of position with such a weak hand is difficult. Against a reasonable opening range, 63o has poor equity (approx 28-30%). Defending here would likely result in losing more chips post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the poor hand quality, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Current stack is 20BB (2000 chips). I hold 8d3d on the button/SB. While suited, this is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against any calling range. My opponent has a massive chip lead and has been applying pressure. Conserving chips for a better spot to push or raise is the priority. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I have been dealt pocket sixes (6d, 6c) on the button. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, is relatively short-stacked with only 1,900 chips remaining (19 BBs). I want to put pressure on the big blind while building a pot with a hand that has significant showdown value. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a solid play here. If Pro moves all-in, I will likely call given the stack sizes and the strength of a pocket pair heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind with 19 BBs (1900 chips) holding J8o. The opponent, who has a massive chip lead, opens for 3x (300). While I need to defend my blind to prevent being run over, J8o is a weak off-suit hand that plays poorly post-flop out of position, especially against a larger 3x raise which offers worse pot odds (calling 200 to win 600, requiring 33% equity realization). Given the stack depth and the risk of bleeding chips against a dominant stack, preserving my 19 BBs for a better spot (like a suited hand, pair, or high cards) to shove or defend is the superior strategy.

I am the Small Blind (Button) with a stack of 1850 chips (18.5 BBs), facing a massive chip leader. My hand is 4c 7c (suited three-gapper), which is a weak starting hand in heads-up play. Given my short stack, I cannot afford to bleed chips by opening with marginal hands that are easily dominated or forced to fold to 3-bets. My opponent has been aggressive, and I need to preserve my stack for hands with better equity (high cards, pairs, or better connectors) to make a stand or steal the blinds. Folding here is the prudent play to maintain fold equity for future shoves or raises.

I hold 9s 7d on the button. Pro is currently playing a very short stack of 1,750 chips (17.5 BB) and has shown a pattern of extreme tightness, even folding premium hands like pocket queens to pre-flop aggression in recent hands. My strategy is to continue applying pressure by raising to 300, which has frequently induced folds from Pro. This allow me to pick up the blinds and maintain my significant chip lead with minimal risk.

I am in the Big Blind with 17.5 BBs (1750 chips) facing a 3x raise (300) from the massive chip leader on the button. My hand, 9c 5c, is a weak suited two-gapper. The 3x raise size offers worse pot odds (2:1) than a standard min-raise, and defending out of position with a hand that struggles to realize equity is -EV. My stack is getting short, but I still have enough chips to wait for a better spot to shove or defend (e.g., high cards, pairs, or better connectors). Historical play shows I have consistently folded weak hands like 9x and small suited cards against this specific raise size. Preserving chips for a higher equity spot is the priority.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 17BBs (1700 chips). My hand, J7o, is a mediocre hand heads-up (roughly top 53%). While I am in position, my stack is in a precarious zone where I cannot afford to bleed chips. My opponent, Flash, has a massive chip lead and has shown a willingness to 3-bet against my button opens (e.g., Hand 115). Opening for a min-raise to 200 risks over 10% of my stack, and if Flash jams (which he can do effectively with a wide range given the stack disparity), I would have to fold. Open-shoving 17BBs with J7o is slightly -EV. I have folded my last few buttons, which might suggest a tight image, but J7o is not strong enough to exploit that risk-free. Preserving my stack for a hand with high-card value (Ax, Kx, Qx) or a pair is the prudent strategy for a comeback.

With J4o on the button, I have a marginal hand in a heads-up situation. However, the Big Blind is extremely short-stacked with only 16 Big Blinds (1600 chips). Given the stack sizes, I can apply pressure by raising. J4o has decent playability if called, and I have the opportunity to pick up the blinds and antes immediately. Raising to 300 (3x) is a standard opening size that puts the short stack in a difficult position where they must either commit a large portion of their stack or fold.

Current stack is 1600 (16 BB). Opponent raises to 300 (3x). I hold J7o in the BB. While I am getting 2:1 pot odds requiring ~33% equity, J7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially with a low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (~2.2 post-flop). I previously folded J8o (Hand #120) and T7o (Hand #116) to similar raises. The opponent is aggressive post-flop, often c-betting, making it difficult to realize equity without hitting the board. Preserving the 16 BB stack for better shoving or defending opportunities is strategically superior to bleeding chips with a marginal holding.

Current stack is 15.5 BBs (1550 chips). Held hand 9s5c is statistically very weak (bottom 15% of hands). In a Heads-Up match, while ranges are wider, 95 offsuit is generally below the threshold for profitable play, especially against a chip leader who has been aggressive. Limping invites a raise which forces a fold, wasting chips. Raising turns the hand into a bluff that will likely be called or re-raised given the stack disparity. The best course of action is to preserve the stack for a hand with better equity or playability.

I have King-Queen suited, which is a powerhouse in heads-up play. My opponent is extremely short-stacked with only 1,450 chips (less than 15 big blinds). I will continue my aggressive strategy of raising from the button, which has led to many folds from the Big Blind in recent hands. If my opponent decides to shove their remaining chips, this hand is strong enough to call.

I hold 3h 7s in the Big Blind with a stack of 14.5 BBs. My opponent raised to 3 BBs. This is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), my equity realization with 73o out of position is extremely poor. Calling would bleed a significant portion (14%) of my short stack with a hand that rarely wins. I need to preserve my chips to shove or defend with a hand that has better equity or playability. Folding is the only viable option here.

I am on the Button with 14BB (1400 chips) and holding King-Eight offsuit. I have been extremely card-dead and passive recently, folding my last 5 buttons and bleeding chips to blinds. K8o is strong enough to play heads-up. While a shove is an option to deny equity, 14BB is slightly deep for a standard shove with this hand, and min-raising to 200 allows me to steal the blinds cheaply or play a pot in position. Given my recent tight image, a min-raise might get credit. If the opponent 3-bet shoves, I can fold and preserve 12BB, whereas shoving risks my entire stack. Therefore, a standard min-raise is the best risk-adjusted play.

I am in the Big Blind with J5o. The opponent, Pro, has a very short stack of only 1250 chips (12.5 big blinds) and has raised to 200. This is a significant portion of his remaining stack. While J5o is a weak hand, I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300 (getting 3:1 odds). However, against a 12.5 BB stack that is raising from the button, I should be cautious of a shove on any flop. Given the pot odds and the fact that I have a massive chip lead (18450 vs 1250), I can afford to see a flop and try to eliminate him if I hit, but calling here is standard to realize some equity against his range.

I have flopped trips with Jacks on a board of Qc-Jc-Js. This is a very strong hand, especially heads-up. Given that Pro has a very short stack (only 12.5 BB remaining) and was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to him. Checking allows him to continue his aggression with bluffs, semi-bluffs (like club draws), or weaker made hands like a Queen. If he bets, I can then decide whether to just call to keep his bluffs in or raise to commit him. Leading out might cause him to fold his air, whereas checking maximizes the chance of him putting more chips in the pot.

I am in position with King-high on a paired board (J-Q-J) that connects well with the Big Blind's calling range (which includes many Qx, Jx, and straight/flush draws). My hand has some showdown value against missed draws but is unlikely to get better hands to fold if I bet, nor will it get value from worse hands. With a short stack of 12.5BB, pot control is crucial. Checking back allows me to realize equity for free, potentially hit a King on the turn, or re-evaluate based on the opponent's turn action without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

I have trips with Jacks, though my kicker (5) is weak. The Ace of Spades on the turn is a card that very likely improves the opponent's pre-flop raising range if they have hands like AK, AQ, or AJ. Having checked the flop to induce bluffs or trap with my strong hand, I will check again on the turn. This allows Pro to bet with an Ace, a Queen, or a straight draw (like clubs or KT). Since Pro has a very short stack of only 12.5 big blinds, I want to give him every opportunity to commit chips to the pot before I potentially move all-in on the river or respond to his bet.

The board [Jc Qc Js As] is extremely coordinated and connects well with the Big Blind's calling range (Ax, Qx, Jx, K-T). Although the Ace is a scare card that favors my range as the pre-flop raiser, it likely improved the opponent if they held any Ax. I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Betting turns my hand into a bluff with little equity if called, and checking back allows me to see a free river to potentially hit my straight or top pair, or win at showdown against missed flush draws. With a 12.5BB stack, preserving chips is critical.

On a board of Jc-Qc-Js-As-Kh, I have trip Jacks with a 5 kicker. This is a very strong hand, but the board is incredibly coordinated. Any Ten makes a straight, and many cards in Pro's pre-flop raising range (like AJ, KJ, QJ, AA, KK, QQ) now beat me. Pro checked both the flop and the turn after being the pre-flop aggressor, which could mean he's either waving the white flag with air or trapping. Given the straight and full house possibilities, and the fact that my kicker is very weak, checking is the most prudent play to get to showdown or see what Pro does with his remaining stack.

On the River, the board is [Jc Qc Js As Kh], giving me Two Pair (Kings and Jacks) with an Ace kicker. The board is very coordinated; any Ten makes a straight, any Jack makes trips, and any Ace beats my two pair. Flash has checked all three streets, which polarizes his range to missed draws, weak made hands (Qx, low pairs), or slow-played monsters/traps (Tx, Jx, Ax). Given that I only beat Qx and bluffs, and worse hands are unlikely to call a bet while better hands (Ax, Tx, Jx) will certainly call or raise, there is no value in betting. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value against Qx or chop with other Kx hands without risking more chips.

I am on the button with a premium hand, Ace-Queen of diamonds. My opponent, Pro, is extremely short-stacked with only 11.5 big blinds remaining (1150 chips). I want to play a large pot or force a decision for his tournament life. A standard raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous aggressive play and puts significant pressure on him while keeping the pot manageable if he chooses to defend. If he moves all-in, I will certainly call given the strength of my hand and the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with 11.5 BBs (1150 chips). The opponent is raising to 300 (3 BBs) very frequently from the button, indicating a very wide range, likely attempting to steal blinds aggressively. My hand, Qh7c, has reasonable equity against a wide opening range. Calling would leave me with less than a pot-sized bet behind and out of position, which is a disadvantageous spot. Folding would preserve 10.5 BBs, but I am bleeding chips rapidly due to the opponent's aggression. Shoving (raising all-in) maximizes my fold equity, potentially taking down the 400-chip pot (nearly 35% of my stack) uncontested if the opponent folds their weakest holdings. If called, Q7o is rarely 'dead' and has about 35-40% equity against a likely calling range. The Combined Fold Equity and Pot Equity make shoving +EV compared to folding or flat-calling.

I have a premium hand (Ace-Queen of diamonds) and my opponent is nearly all-in, having raised to 1150 with only 100 chips left behind. Given the strength of my hand and the pot odds, I am committed to this pot. I will raise to 1250 to put Pro all-in and see all five cards. I am likely ahead or in a coin-flip situation against his range, and with the stack advantage I have, this is a mandatory call or raise.

I have previously committed 1150 chips, leaving me with just 100 chips behind. The opponent has put me all-in for the remaining 100 chips. The pot is currently 2400 chips, and I only need to call 100 more. This gives me pot odds of 24:1. With Q7o, I have significantly more than the ~4% equity required to make this call profitable. Even against the strongest possible range (AA), I have over 10% equity. Therefore, folding is impossible, and I must call the remaining amount.
